ranges.reason: Introduction: what is a body? Sets up the episode's theme.
Modern vs. ancient view of body; God's body before incarnation.
Ancient understanding: body as nexus of potentialities/collection of powers.
Body parts as powers: eyes (sight), ears (hearing), feet (movement), arms (strength), face (communication).
These are not metaphors; God's body parts are real powers.
Scripture references: God's arm, eyes, face; Moses speaking face to face; you cannot see God's face and live.
Modern tendency to treat these as anthropomorphisms; the show argues the opposite.
Humans are theomorphic; God is the paradigm; example from Ephesians 5.
Face of God in OT; Jonah fleeing from God's face; Mount Sinai.
Critique of anthropomorphism; Christian teaching is that humans are like God, not vice versa.
Summary: body as nexus of potentialities; God has these abilities.
Distinction between body (soma) and flesh (sarx) in NT; Greek terms.
Paul's careful use of terms; body vs. flesh not synonymous.
Flesh is a subcategory of body; weakened, corruptible, mortal.
Adam and Eve's change after sin: garments of skin; body as collection of powers altered.
Word became flesh (not just body); Christ's resurrection body has flesh and bone.
Origin of modern view: pagan Greek philosophy; God of philosophers vs. God of Scripture.
Aristotle's prime mover: thought thinking itself, unaware of creation; leads to deism.
God of philosophers has no body (no communication, no action); contrast with biblical God.
Line from philosopher's God to deism to secular materialism; pastoral and theological importance.
Synod of the Oak: accusation against St. John Chrysostom of being an originist; anthropomorphites.
Accusation related to Christology; originist view that God has no body leads to Nestorianism.
Connection to divine energies; origin's view incompatible with Orthodox Christology.
Nestorius's solution: two persons conjoined by good will; necessary because God has no body.
Term 'lordly man'; Apollinarianism; St. Cyril; Christ has two wills and energies.
Caller Greg: bodies of fallen sons of God and Nephilim; answer: angels have bodies, Nephilim spirits seek bodies.
OT theophanies: word of Yahweh appeared to Abraham, Samuel, Jeremiah; God stands, touches, eats.
Oak of Mamre: Yahweh appears as a man; Yahweh talks to Yahweh; shows plurality in God.
Exodus 33: Moses speaks face to face; no one can see God's face and live; identifies the Son as the visible Yahweh.
Caller Scott: who is interacting in OT? Answer: Yahweh is a title applicable to all Trinity; the Son is the visible one; Father and Spirit also have bodies.
Pagan gods have multiple bodies; idolatry as creating a body for a god; contrast with one place for Yahweh.
Torah forbids idolatry and commands one place of worship; God chooses the place; you go to Him.
Day of Atonement: high priest enters Holy of Holies; incense to cover God's appearance; God appears on that day.
Modern analog: Holy Fire; Christ's atonement makes it safe to approach God.
Isaiah and Ezekiel see God's body in temple; Ezekiel sees God leave; now any place with God's body is a temple.
Teaser: does Christ have two bodies? (to be continued).
Conclusion: God has bodily existence; we worship the God who interacts; we are theomorphic.
St. Paul at Areopagus: God is not far; we live and move in Him; God loves us and wants to be found.

Restatement of modern view.
Preview of next episode; warning about brain bending; filler.
Banter about brain recovery; meme discussion.
Banter about memes and Star Trek.
Clarification that it's not metaphor; already covered.
Transition filler.
Restatement of modern view.
Restatement of point.
Transition.
Restatement.
Banter about reading glasses; off-topic.
Filler.

Break; sponsor messages; station ID.
Return from break; recap; filler.
Transition.
Transition to caller.
Caller Alex: question and answer about theosis; mostly kept but some pleasantries and teasers dropped.
Restatement of philosopher's God.
Transition.
Filler about accusation.

Transition.
